content:
# Genesis Chapter 45 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 26v–27r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_045_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_045_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 45–46 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0049 | https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0050

---

*Joseph Cannot Control Himself — "I Am Joseph!" (vv. 1–3)*

**45:1** And Joseph was not able to control himself [וְלֹא-יָכֹל יוֹסֵף לְהִתְאַפֵּק] before all who stood before him [לְכֹל הַנִּצָּבִים עָלָיו] — and he called out [וַיִּקְרָא]: Send everyone away from me [הוֹצִיאוּ כָל-אִישׁ מֵעָלָי] — and no man stood with him [וְלֹא-עָמַד אִישׁ אִתּוֹ] when Joseph made himself known [בְּהִתְוַדַּע יוֹסֵף] to his brothers [אֶל-אֶחָיו].

**45:2** And he gave forth his voice [וַיִּתֵּן אֶת-קֹלוֹ] in weeping [בִּבְכִי] — and Egypt heard [וַיִּשְׁמְעוּ מִצְרַיִם] and the house of Pharaoh heard [וַיִּשְׁמַע בֵּית פַּרְעֹה].

**45:3** And Joseph said to his brothers [וַיֹּאמֶר יוֹסֵף אֶל-אֶחָיו]: I am Joseph [אֲנִי יוֹסֵף] — is my father still alive [הַעוֹד אָבִי חָי] — and his brothers were not able to answer him [וְלֹא-יָכְלוּ אֶחָיו לַעֲנוֹת אֹתוֹ] — for they were terrified before him [כִּי נִבְהֲלוּ מִפָּנָיו].

---

*Joseph's Theology of Providence (vv. 4–8)*

**45:4** And Joseph said to his brothers [וַיֹּאמֶר יוֹסֵף אֶל-אֶחָיו]: Draw near to me [גְּשׁוּ-נָא אֵלַי] — and they drew near [וַיִּגָּשׁוּ] — and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: I am Joseph your brother [אֲנִי יוֹסֵף אֲחִיכֶם] — whom you sold into Egypt [אֲשֶׁר-מְכַרְתֶּם אֹתִי מִצְרָיְמָה].

**45:5** And now [וְעַתָּה] — do not be grieved [אַל-תֵּעָצְבוּ] and do not let anger burn in your eyes [וְאַל-יִחַר בְּעֵינֵיכֶם] that you sold me here [כִּי-מְכַרְתֶּם אֹתִי הֵנָּה] — for to preserve life [כִּי לְמִחְיָה] God sent me before you [שְׁלָחַנִי אֱלֹהִים לִפְנֵיכֶם].

**45:6** For these two years [כִּי-זֶה שְׁנָתַיִם הָרָעָב] the famine has been in the midst of the land [בְּקֶרֶב הָאָרֶץ] — and still there are five years [וְעוֹד חָמֵשׁ שָׁנִים] in which there will be no plowing or harvest [אֲשֶׁר אֵין-חָרִישׁ וְקָצִיר].

**45:7** And God sent me before you [וַיִּשְׁלָחֵנִי אֱלֹהִים לִפְנֵיכֶם] to set for you a remnant in the land [לָשׂוּם לָכֶם שְׁאֵרִית בָּאָרֶץ] and to keep you alive [וּלְהַחֲיוֹת לָכֶם] as a great deliverance [לִפְלֵיטָה גְדֹלָה].

**45:8** And now [וְעַתָּה] — it was not you who sent me here [לֹא-אַתֶּם שְׁלַחְתֶּם אֹתִי הֵנָּה] — but God [כִּי הָאֱלֹהִים] — and he has set me as a father to Pharaoh [וַיְשִׂימֵנִי לְאָב לְפַרְעֹה] and as lord over all his house [וּלְאָדוֹן לְכָל-בֵּיתוֹ] and ruler over all the land of Egypt [וּמֹשֵׁל בְּכָל-אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם].

---

*Summons to Jacob — Come Down to Egypt, to Goshen (vv. 9–13)*

**45:9** Hurry [מַהֲרוּ] and go up to my father [וַעֲלוּ אֶל-אָבִי] and say to him [וַאֲמַרְתֶּם אֵלָיו]: Thus says your son Joseph [כֹּה אָמַר בִּנְךָ יוֹסֵף] — God has set me as lord over all Egypt [שָׂמַנִי אֱלֹהִים לְאָדוֹן לְכָל-מִצְרָיִם] — come down to me [רֵדָה אֵלַי] — do not delay [אַל-תַּעֲמֹד].

**45:10** And you shall dwell [וְיָשַׁבְתָּ] in the land of Goshen [בְּאֶרֶץ-גֹּשֶׁן] and you shall be near to me [וְהָיִיתָ קָרוֹב אֵלַי] — you and your sons [אַתָּה וּבָנֶיךָ] and your sons' sons [וּבְנֵי בָנֶיךָ] and your flocks and your herds [וְצֹאנְךָ וּבְקָרֶךָ] and all that is yours [וְכָל-אֲשֶׁר-לָךְ].

**45:11** And I will sustain you there [וְכִלְכַּלְתִּי אֹתְךָ שָׁם] — for still five years of famine [כִּי-עוֹד חָמֵשׁ שָׁנִים רָעָב] — lest you and your household [פֶּן-תִּוָּרֵשׁ אַתָּה וּבֵיתְךָ] and all that is yours [וְכָל-אֲשֶׁר-לָךְ] come to poverty [פֶּן-תִּוָּרֵשׁ].

**45:12** And behold [וְהִנֵּה] — your eyes see [עֵינֵיכֶם רֹאוֹת] and the eyes of my brother Benjamin [וְעֵינֵי אָחִי בִנְיָמִן] that it is my mouth [כִּי-פִי] that is speaking to you [הַמְדַבֵּר אֲלֵיכֶם].

**45:13** And you shall tell my father [וְהִגַּדְתֶּם לְאָבִי] all my glory in Egypt [אֶת-כָּל-כְּבוֹדִי בְמִצְרַיִם] and all that you have seen [וְאֵת כָּל-אֲשֶׁר רְאִיתֶם] — and you shall hurry [וּמִהַרְתֶּם] and bring down [וְהוֹרַדְתֶּם] my father here [אֶת-אָבִי הֵנָּה].

---

*Joseph Weeps — He Kisses All His Brothers (vv. 14–15)*

**45:14** And he fell upon the neck of Benjamin his brother [וַיִּפֹּל עַל-צַוְּארֵי בִנְיָמִן-אָחִיו] and wept [וַיֵּבְךְּ] — and Benjamin wept [וּבִנְיָמִן בָּכָה] upon his neck [עַל-צַוָּארָיו].

**45:15** And he kissed all his brothers [וַיְנַשֵּׁק לְכָל-אֶחָיו] and wept over them [וַיֵּבְךְּ עֲלֵיהֶם] — and after that [וְאַחֲרֵי כֵן] his brothers spoke with him [דִּבְּרוּ אֶחָיו אִתּוֹ].

---

*Pharaoh's Invitation — The Best of Egypt (vv. 16–20)*

**45:16** And the report was heard [וְהַקֹּל נִשְׁמַע] in the house of Pharaoh [בֵּית פַּרְעֹה] saying [לֵאמֹר]: Joseph's brothers have come [בָּאוּ אֲחֵי יוֹסֵף] — and it was good in Pharaoh's eyes [וַיִּיטַב בְּעֵינֵי פַרְעֹה] and in the eyes of his servants [וּבְעֵינֵי עֲבָדָיו].

**45:17** And Pharaoh said to Joseph [וַיֹּאמֶר פַּרְעֹה אֶל-יוֹסֵף]: Say to your brothers [אֱמֹר אֶל-אַחֶיךָ]: Do this [זֹאת עֲשׂוּ] — load your animals [טַעֲנוּ אֶת-בְּעִירְכֶם] and go [וּלְכוּ] — come back [בֹּאוּ] to the land of Canaan [אַרְצָה כְּנָעַן].

**45:18** And take your father [וּקְחוּ אֶת-אֲבִיכֶם] and your households [וְאֶת-בָּתֵּיכֶם] and come to me [וּבֹאוּ אֵלַי] — and I will give you [וְאֶתְּנָה לָכֶם] the good of the land of Egypt [אֶת-טוּב אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם] — and you shall eat [וְאִכְלוּ] the fat of the land [אֶת-חֵלֶב הָאָרֶץ].

**45:19** And you [וְאַתָּה] — you are commanded [צֻוֵּיתָה] — do this [זֹאת עֲשׂוּ]: Take for yourselves from the land of Egypt [קְחוּ-לָכֶם מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם] wagons [עֲגָלוֹת] for your little ones [לְטַפְּכֶם] and for your wives [וְלִנְשֵׁיכֶם] — and carry your father [וּנְשָׂאתֶם אֶת-אֲבִיכֶם] and come [וּבָאתֶם].

**45:20** And let not your eye spare [וְעֵינְכֶם אַל-תָּחֹס] your belongings [עַל-כְּלֵיכֶם] — for the good of all the land of Egypt [כִּי-טוּב כָּל-אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם] — it is yours [לָכֶם הוּא].

---

*Provisions for the Journey — Benjamin's Portion (vv. 21–23)*

**45:21** And the sons of Israel did so [וַיַּעֲשׂוּ-כֵן בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל] — and Joseph gave them wagons [וַיִּתֵּן לָהֶם יוֹסֵף עֲגָלוֹת] according to the command of Pharaoh [עַל-פִּי פַרְעֹה] — and he gave them provisions [וַיִּתֵּן לָהֶם צֵדָה] for the journey [לַדָּרֶךְ].

**45:22** To each of them he gave [לְכֻלָּם נָתַן] changes of garments [חֲלִפוֹת שְׂמָלֹת] — and to Benjamin [וּלְבִנְיָמִן] he gave [נָתַן] three hundred pieces of silver [שְׁלֹשׁ מֵאוֹת כֶּסֶף] and five changes of garments [וְחָמֵשׁ חֲלִפֹת שְׂמָלֹת].

**45:23** And to his father he sent [וּלְאָבִיו שָׁלַח] thus [כְּזֹאת] — ten donkeys [עֲשָׂרָה חֲמֹרִים] carrying [נֹשְׂאִים] from the good things of Egypt [מִטּוּב מִצְרָיִם] and ten female donkeys [וְעֶשֶׂר אֲתֹנֹת] carrying [נֹשְׂאֹת] grain and bread and provisions [בָּר וָלֶחֶם וּמָזוֹן] for his father [לְאָבִיו] for the journey [לַדָּרֶךְ].

---

*"Do Not Quarrel on the Way" — The Brothers Depart (vv. 24–25)*

**45:24** And he sent his brothers away [וַיְשַׁלַּח אֶת-אֶחָיו] and they went [וַיֵּלֵכוּ] — and he said to them [וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם]: Do not quarrel [אַל-תִּרְגְּזוּ] on the way [בַּדָּרֶךְ].

**45:25** And they went up from Egypt [וַיַּעֲלוּ מִמִּצְרָיִם] and they came [וַיָּבֹאוּ] to the land of Canaan [אֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן] to Jacob their father [אֶל-יַעֲקֹב אֲבִיהֶם].

---

*The Brothers Tell Jacob — "Joseph Is Still Alive!" (vv. 26–28)*

**45:26** And they told him [וַיַּגִּידוּ לוֹ] saying [לֵאמֹר]: Joseph is still alive [עוֹד יוֹסֵף חָי] — and that he is ruler [וְכִי-הוּא מֹשֵׁל] over all the land of Egypt [בְּכָל-אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם] — and his heart went numb [וַיָּפָג לִבּוֹ] — for he did not believe them [כִּי לֹא-הֶאֱמִין לָהֶם].

**45:27** And they spoke to him [וַיְדַבְּרוּ אֵלָיו] all the words of Joseph [אֵת כָּל-דִּבְרֵי יוֹסֵף] which he had spoken to them [אֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר אֲלֵהֶם] — and he saw the wagons [וַיַּרְא אֶת-הָעֲגָלוֹת] which Joseph had sent [אֲשֶׁר-שָׁלַח יוֹסֵף] to carry him [לָשֵׂאת אֹתוֹ] — and the spirit of Jacob their father revived [וַתְּחִי רוּחַ יַעֲקֹב אֲבִיהֶם].

**45:28** And Israel said [וַיֹּאמֶר יִשְׂרָאֵל]: Enough [רַב] — Joseph my son is still alive [עוֹד יוֹסֵף בְּנִי חָי] — I will go and see him [אֵלְכָה וְאֶרְאֶנּוּ] before I die [בְּטֶרֶם אָמוּת].

---

## Philological Notes

### 45:1 — לֹא-יָכֹל יוֹסֵף לְהִתְאַפֵּק (lo yakol Yosef l'hit'appeq)
"Joseph was not able to control himself." The verb הִתְאַפֵּק (hit'appeq, Hitpael of אָפַק, "to restrain, hold back, contain") appeared in its positive form in 43:31: וַיִּתְאַפַּק (vayyit'appaq, "and he controlled himself"). There Joseph succeeded in restraining himself — he washed his face, came out, and masked his emotion for the duration of the meal. Here the same root appears with the negation לֹא-יָכֹל ("was not able") — the dam breaks. The Hitpael form is reflexive: it is not others who restrain Joseph, but Joseph who restrains himself. His will has reached its limit. The word conveys muscular effort — not a sudden explosion but the collapse of something held under great pressure. The collapse happens specifically "before all who stood before him" (לְכֹל הַנִּצָּבִים עָלָיו) — a large room of attendants, servants, court officials. Joseph must empty it. This is a private moment for which public Egypt has no place.

### 45:3 — אֲנִי יוֹסֵף (ani Yosef)
"I am Joseph." Three Hebrew words. The two-word statement אֲנִי יוֹסֵף is the axis around which the entire Joseph narrative turns. The brothers have stood before this man twice — bowed before him (42:6, 43:26) in unconscious fulfillment of the dream they once mocked (37:5-8) — without recognizing him. Now the Egyptian disguise — the shaved face, the linen robes, the court language spoken through an interpreter (42:23) — dissolves. The name יוֹסֵף is the one he was given by his mother Rachel: "God has gathered away my reproach" — יָסַף יְהוָה, "may the LORD add" (30:24). The brevity of the self-disclosure is shattering. Between "I am Joseph" and "is my father still alive?" — there is no explanation, no recrimination, no long speech. Just the name and the father. The question about the father (הַעוֹד אָבִי חָי, "is my father still alive?") is identical to the question Joseph asked through the steward in 43:7 and that the brothers themselves asked in 44:19 — but now Joseph asks it in his own voice, for himself, without pretense. The brothers' inability to answer (לֹא-יָכְלוּ לַעֲנוֹת, "they were not able to answer") mirrors Joseph's own inability to control himself a verse earlier: the word יָכֹל appears in both clauses, binding the two paralyzed parties.

### 45:5 — לְמִחְיָה שְׁלָחַנִי אֱלֹהִים לִפְנֵיכֶם (l'michyah shelachani Elohim lifneichem)
"For to preserve life God sent me before you." The noun מִחְיָה (michyah) is derived from the root חָיָה (chayah, "to live") and means "sustenance of life, that which keeps alive, preservation of life." It is a word that encompasses both the physical provision (food that keeps one alive) and the act of preservation itself (the keeping-alive of persons). The theological claim is staggering: the brothers' act of selling Joseph — their deliberate wickedness — was in fact God's act of sending. The verb שָׁלַח (shalach, "to send") is the same verb used throughout these chapters for human sending: Jacob sent Joseph to his brothers (37:14), Joseph's brothers sent him to Egypt through the Midianites. Here Joseph reclaims the verb and reattributes it: the agent was not the brothers but God. The phrase לִפְנֵיכֶם (lifneichem, "before you, ahead of you") implies a going-ahead, a preparation in advance — Joseph was sent forward in time and space to be there when the crisis arrived. This is the theological center of the Joseph narrative.

### 45:7 — שְׁאֵרִית בָּאָרֶץ וּלְפֵלֵיטָה גְדֹלָה (she'erith ba'aretz ul'fleitah gedolah)
"A remnant in the land and a great deliverance." Two closely related words articulate the scope of Joseph's mission. שְׁאֵרִית (she'erith) means "remainder, remnant, what is left over" — from the root שָׁאַר (sha'ar, "to remain"). This is not merely "survival" in a generic sense but the preservation of the specific community — Jacob's family, the covenant line, the people of promise. פְּלֵיטָה (pleitah) means "escape, deliverance, those who have escaped" — from פָּלַט (palat, "to escape, to deliver"). The two words together — remnant and escape — are the vocabulary of later biblical redemption theology: the remnant that survives catastrophe is the seed of future restoration. These words will become the vocabulary of the prophets for Israel's survival through exile. Joseph here speaks, without knowing it, in the register of Isaianic promise (see Isa 37:31-32: "The surviving remnant of the house of Judah... shall again take root downward and bear fruit upward").

### 45:8 — לֹא-אַתֶּם שְׁלַחְתֶּם אֹתִי הֵנָּה כִּי הָאֱלֹהִים (lo attem shelachtem oti henna ki ha-Elohim)
"It was not you who sent me here, but God." The construction is emphatic fronting: לֹא-אַתֶּם ("not you") is placed before the verb, creating a cleft sentence — "it was not you who..." The contrast with כִּי הָאֱלֹהִים ("but God") is absolute. Joseph does not say "you sent me and God used it" — he says the sending was God's. The article on הָאֱלֹהִים ("the God," emphatic) focuses the attribution: not some god, not divine fate in general, but the God. The three-part titulature that follows — father to Pharaoh, lord over all his house, ruler over all Egypt — demonstrates that divine sending accomplished something humanly impossible: the Hebrew slave is now in a position of absolute power within the most powerful empire on earth. Joseph's theological reframing is not naive exculpation of his brothers (he will return to the reality of what they did in 50:20) but a statement about the primary agent behind the history. LXX: οὐχ ὑμεῖς με ἀπεστάλκατε ὡσαύτως, ἀλλ' ὁ θεός — "it was not you who sent me, in like manner, but God."

### 45:9 — אָב לְפַרְעֹה (av l'Pharaoh)
"A father to Pharaoh." The title אָב (av, "father") for a royal counselor or vizier is attested in Ancient Near Eastern titulature. In Egypt, the phrase "father of Pharaoh" or "god's father" (it-netjer) was a real honorific title for senior palace officials. Joseph uses it here in a general way to indicate his status as senior advisor and protector of the king — a role analogous to a father's guidance of a son. The Hebrew title appears elsewhere in the Joseph formula given by Pharaoh in 41:43-44, where Joseph is given the signet ring, fine linen garments, and the gold chain — the accoutrements of the Egyptian vizier. The full trio of titles — father to Pharaoh, lord over all his house, ruler over all the land of Egypt — recapitulates the three elements of the investiture in 41:41-44.

### 45:10 — אֶרֶץ גֹּשֶׁן (eretz Goshen)
"The land of Goshen." Goshen appears here for the first time as the specific territory designated for Jacob's family. Goshen (גֹּשֶׁן) is generally identified with the Wadi Tumilat region in the northeastern Nile Delta — a fertile area suited for pastoral settlement, close enough to the Delta residence of Pharaoh for Joseph to "be near" but distinct from the Egyptian heartland. The name may derive from an Egyptian toponym; it does not appear in Egyptian records under this Hebrew form, but the identification with the eastern Delta is consistent with Egyptian archaeological and geographical knowledge. The promise "you shall be near to me" (קָרוֹב אֵלַי) reflects Joseph's pastoral care — not merely physical proximity but the security of being under his protection. The listing of "sons, sons' sons, flocks and herds" is the complete enumeration of a patriarch's household — the entire covenant family entering under Joseph's provision.

### 45:22 — חֲלִפוֹת שְׂמָלֹת (chalifot semalot)
"Changes of garments." The noun חֲלִיפָה (chalifah, "change, exchange, set of replacement garments") from the root חָלַף (chalaf, "to pass through, to change, to renew") denotes a set of garments given as a gift or prize, not for immediate wear but as a valuable exchange commodity. The giving of chalifot is a gesture of honor and distinction — Naomi will tell Ruth she has sent a change of garments (Ruth 3:3 uses the same root). Samson promises thirty chalifot of garments as a wager (Judg 14:12-13). In the ancient Near East, fine garments were wealth — portable, valuable, easily exchanged. The garment motif that began in Genesis 37 with the coat of many colors (37:3), continued through Potiphar's wife tearing Joseph's cloak (39:12), through the prison garments stripped off when Joseph appeared before Pharaoh (41:14), through Joseph's fine Egyptian linen investiture (41:42) — now reaches its redemptive inversion: the same Joseph stripped of garments now distributes garments to all his brothers. Benjamin receives five chalifot and three hundred silver — five being the numerical mark of honor (five times as much, 43:34; five years of famine, 45:6; Benjamin's portion at the meal was five, 43:34). The number echoes throughout the Joseph-Benjamin relationship.

### 45:24 — אַל-תִּרְגְּזוּ בַּדָּרֶךְ (al-tirgezu baderekh)
"Do not quarrel on the way." The verb רָגַז (ragaz) is rich in semantic range: (1) to tremble, to quake (of the earth, of persons in terror); (2) to be agitated, disturbed, in turmoil; (3) to quarrel, to stir up strife. The root appears in contexts of both physical shaking (Isa 64:2) and interpersonal conflict. Joseph's parting word to his brothers carries deliberate ambiguity: do not quarrel (with each other about who was most guilty) and do not be troubled/agitated (by what has just happened). The brothers have just been identified, their past crime referenced (v.4-5), and forgiven. There is now much to process — and the journey home is long. Joseph anticipates that the combination of guilt, relief, shock, and the need to assign blame could tear the brothers apart before they reach their father. The command is protective: he sends them on their way with a word that seals the reconciliation against corrosive reopening. LXX: μὴ ὀργίζεσθε ἐν τῇ ὁδῷ — "do not be angry on the way" — using ὀργίζομαι (to be angry), capturing one semantic layer; but MT's רָגַז covers both "be agitated/afraid" and "quarrel," giving Joseph's word a wider protective force.

### 45:26 — וַיָּפָג לִבּוֹ (vayyafag libbo)
"And his heart went numb." The verb פּוּג (pug) is rare — it appears only here and in Ps 77:3 (where it means "my spirit grew faint"). The root carries the sense of becoming numb, going cold, failing. The idiom וַיָּפָג לִבּוֹ ("and his heart went numb") describes a sudden failure of the heart's ability to respond — a kind of stunned suspension of belief. This is not grief, not joy, not anger — it is the collapse of cognitive function before an impossible report. Jacob's heart "goes numb" (froze, went cold, failed to beat) because the words "Joseph is alive" cannot be processed. Twenty-two years of mourning (from ch.37 to this point) have built a fixed reality: Joseph is dead. The news does not fit. The heart cannot hold it. Only when the physical evidence arrives — the wagons — does the heart recover (v.27: וַתְּחִי רוּחַ יַעֲקֹב, "and the spirit of Jacob revived"). LXX: ἐξέλιπεν ἡ ψυχὴ αὐτοῦ — "his soul gave out / his soul fainted" — using ἐκλείπω (to fail, to give out, to faint), which captures the sense well, though it describes a more complete failure than MT's "numbing."

### 45:27 — וַתְּחִי רוּחַ יַעֲקֹב אֲבִיהֶם (vattechi ruach Ya'aqov avihem)
"And the spirit of Jacob their father revived." The verb חָיָה (chayah, "to live, to revive") with רוּחַ (ruach, "spirit, breath, wind") as subject is the exact idiom for revival from near-death. The same root — חָיָה — is the word used for the life-preservation mission Joseph announced in verses 5 and 7 (מִחְיָה, "preservation of life"). The narrator closes the loop: the entire mission of divine sending was לְמִחְיָה — to preserve life — and now the first life it preserves is the very life it was meant most immediately to preserve: Jacob's. The spirit (רוּחַ) of Jacob had, in effect, been dying since Genesis 37 — Jacob's grief was existential, not merely emotional. Judah described it: if Benjamin does not return, the gray hair will go down in grief to Sheol (44:29, 44:31). The wagons — physical, material evidence of Joseph's Egyptian power — are what the word alone could not accomplish. Jacob's revival (וַתְּחִי) is the micro-fulfillment of what Joseph was sent to do. The chapter ends on this word: חִי — "alive."

### 45:28 — רַב (rav)
"Enough." The single word רַב (rav, "much, great, sufficient, enough") stands alone as Jacob's first response upon revival — a remarkable syntactic isolation. It is not an interjection in the usual sense but a declaration: "It is enough, it is sufficient, it is great." The same word carries other meanings — "too much," "great," "many" — but here in its exclamatory use it means: this one thing (Joseph alive) outweighs everything. Jacob does not say "I am happy" or "I cannot believe it." He says: "Enough." All the years of mourning, all the accumulated grief — it is answered in this one fact. The sentence that follows is among the most quietly monumental in Genesis: עוֹד יוֹסֵף בְּנִי חָי ("Joseph my son is still alive") — and then the declaration of intention: אֵלְכָה וְאֶרְאֶנּוּ בְּטֶרֶם אָמוּת ("I will go and see him before I die"). The narrative of the Joseph story — the decades of his absence from the biblical text as a son who is with his father — closes with this sentence. The father who refused to be comforted (37:35) is now resolved to journey toward the impossible comfort that has become real.

---

## LXX Comparison Table (Vaticanus, pp. 45–46)

| Verse | MT (Hebrew) | LXX (Vaticanus Greek) | Significant Divergence |
|-------|-------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| 45:1 | לֹא-יָכֹל יוֹסֵף לְהִתְאַפֵּק — "Joseph was not able to control himself" | οὐκ ἠδύνατο Ἰωσὴφ ἀνέχεσθαι — "Joseph was not able to contain himself" | LXX uses ἀνέχομαι (to hold up, to contain, to bear/endure); MT's הִתְאַפֵּק is more specifically "to restrain oneself by an act of will"; LXX's ἀνέχεσθαι describes passive containment |
| 45:3 | נִבְהֲלוּ מִפָּנָיו — "they were terrified before him" | ἐταράχθησαν — "they were disturbed/thrown into turmoil" | LXX uses ταράσσω (to disturb, trouble, throw into confusion); MT's נִבְהַל (nivhal) specifically means "to be terrified, alarmed, in sudden dread" — a stronger word than LXX's disturbance |
| 45:5 | לְמִחְיָה שְׁלָחַנִי אֱלֹהִים — "for to preserve life God sent me" | εἰς γὰρ ζωὴν ἀπέσταλκέν με ὁ θεὸς — "for God has sent me for life" | LXX uses the standard εἰς ζωήν ("for life / into life"); MT's מִחְיָה is more specific — "sustenance of life, life-preservation" — not merely "life" in the abstract; LXX simplifies |
| 45:7 | לְפֵלֵיטָה גְדֹלָה — "as a great deliverance" | ὥστε τραφῆναι ὑμῶν κατάλειμμα μέγα ἐπὶ γῆς — "so that a great remnant of you might be fed upon the earth" | LXX expands and recasts: instead of MT's two nouns (remnant / great deliverance), LXX gives an infinitive clause about feeding; LXX conflates the two concepts; MT's two distinct words (שְׁאֵרִית + פְּלֵיטָה) are preserved in MT |
| 45:8 | לְאָב לְפַרְעֹה — "as a father to Pharaoh" | πατέρα Φαραω — "father of Pharaoh" | Near-exact equivalence; LXX renders literally with genitive construction; the honorific title is preserved in both traditions |
| 45:9 | אַל-תַּעֲמֹד — "do not delay / do not stand" | μὴ μείνῃς — "do not remain / do not stay" | LXX uses μένω (to remain, to stay); MT's עָמַד (amad, "to stand") implies a momentary standing still in hesitation; both capture the urgency of the summons |
| 45:12 | כִּי-פִי הַמְדַבֵּר אֲלֵיכֶם — "that it is my mouth speaking to you" | ὅτι τὸ στόμα μου τὸ λαλοῦν πρὸς ὑμᾶς — "that it is my mouth speaking to you" | Exact equivalence; LXX preserves the emphatic self-identification precisely |
| 45:22 | חֲלִפוֹת שְׂמָלֹת — "changes of garments" | δύο στολάς — "two robes/sets of garments" | Significant divergence: MT gives each brother one chalifah (one set of replacement garments); LXX gives each two robes (δύο στολάς); LXX inflates the gift, possibly through scribal harmonization toward the Benjamin gift |
| 45:23 | בָּר וָלֶחֶם וּמָזוֹן — "grain and bread and provisions" | σῖτον καὶ ἄρτους καὶ τροφὴν — "grain and bread and food" | Near-exact equivalence; LXX renders מָזוֹן (mazon, "food/provisions") as τροφή (food/nourishment); both enumerate three categories of provisions |
| 45:24 | אַל-תִּרְגְּזוּ בַּדָּרֶךְ — "do not quarrel / be agitated on the way" | μὴ ὀργίζεσθε ἐν τῇ ὁδῷ — "do not be angry on the way" | LXX narrows to ὀργίζομαι (to be angry/wrathful); MT's רָגַז includes trembling, agitation, and quarreling — a richer semantic field; MT's ambiguity (fear vs. conflict) is lost in LXX |
| 45:26 | וַיָּפָג לִבּוֹ — "and his heart went numb" | ἐξέλιπεν ἡ ψυχὴ αὐτοῦ — "his soul gave out / fainted" | LXX uses ψυχή (soul) for MT's לֵב (heart/mind); LXX's ἐκλείπω (to give out, to faint) captures complete failing; MT's פּוּג (pug) is a more specific numbing/going-cold — both describe cognitive-emotional paralysis |
| 45:27 | וַתְּחִי רוּחַ יַעֲקֹב אֲבִיהֶם — "and the spirit of Jacob their father revived" | ἀνεζωπύρησεν τὸ πνεῦμα Ἰακὼβ τοῦ πατρὸς αὐτῶν — "and the spirit of Jacob their father was rekindled" | LXX uses ἀναζωπυρέω (to rekindle a fire, to fan a flame back to life) — a vivid metaphor of a dying ember brought back to flame; MT's חָיָה (chayah, "to live, to revive") is the standard revival idiom; LXX's "rekindling" is more pictorial |
| 45:28 | רַב — "Enough" | μέγα μοί ἐστιν — "It is great to me" | LXX expands the single word רַב into a full clause — "It is great to me" (μέγα μοί ἐστιν); MT's isolated רַב has maximum rhetorical weight through its brevity; LXX interprets one semantic range of רַב (= "great/much") but loses the exclamatory isolation |

---

## Structural Note

Genesis 45 is the resolution of the long arc that began in Genesis 37 — the pivot on which the entire Joseph narrative turns. It is the chapter of revelation: identity disclosed, guilt addressed, and providence named.

**The architecture of disclosure (vv.1-8):** The chapter opens with Joseph's loss of control — the body breaking through the will. The structure of the revelation is concentric: Joseph clears the room (v.1) → weeping heard by Egypt and Pharaoh's house (v.2) → "I am Joseph" — is my father alive? (v.3) → "come near" — I am Joseph your brother whom you sold (v.4) → do not grieve because God sent me (v.5) → two years of famine, five more to come (v.6) → God sent me to preserve life / remnant / great deliverance (v.7) → it was not you but God — and here is what God has done (v.8). The movement is: identity → family guilt → theological reframe → scope of mission → full theological statement + Joseph's current position. Joseph does not rush to comfort; he moves methodically through the layers of meaning.

**"I am Joseph" (v.3) and the great reversal:** The three Hebrew words אֲנִי יוֹסֵף stand at the center of the entire Joseph narrative. Every verse from 37:1 to 44:34 has been building toward this declaration, and every verse from 45:4 to 50:26 will unfold from it. The two words echo structurally with divine self-disclosure: in Exodus 3:14, God says to Moses אֶהְיֶה אֲשֶׁר אֶהְיֶה ("I am who I am") and אֶהְיֶה ("I AM") — a disclosure of identity that reorganizes all subsequent events. Joseph's disclosure follows the same grammar of revelation: the name announced, the silence of those before it, the reshaping of all that came before.

**The garment motif completed (v.22):** The giving of chalifot semalot — changes of garments — to each brother is not incidental. The garment narrative of Genesis: Joseph's coat stripped off (37:23) → Joseph's garment grabbed by Potiphar's wife (39:12) → Joseph's prison garments removed before Pharaoh (41:14) → Joseph dressed in fine linen by Pharaoh (41:42) → Benjamin's five changes of garments at the feast (43:34 in portion size; now five actual chalifot) → all brothers given chalifot. The narrator has tracked the garment motif through seven stages, and the seventh is the giving-back. What was stripped is now given. The brother who was stripped is now the giver. Garments in Genesis mark status, honor, betrayal, and restoration — and the distribution here marks the full arc of Joseph's restoration.

**The providence theology (vv.5-8):** Joseph's three-part theological statement is the hermeneutical key to the entire Joseph narrative and prefigures one of the Bible's central claims about divine sovereignty over human evil:
- v.5: "God sent me before you" (divine agency positive)
- v.7: "God sent me before you to set a remnant and a great deliverance" (divine purpose specified)
- v.8: "It was not you but God" (human agency displaced by divine primary causation)

This is not a denial that the brothers acted — Joseph explicitly names their action ("whom you sold," v.4). It is an assertion about ultimate causation and about the direction of history. The narrative does not resolve the tension between human guilt and divine purpose; it holds both. The brothers did evil; God did good through the evil. Joseph's statement in 50:20 will crystallize this: "You intended evil against me — God intended it for good."

**Jacob's dying and revival (vv.26-28):** The death-and-revival sequence of Jacob in these verses is the narrative's preparation for the great migration of Genesis 46. Jacob's "heart going numb" (v.26) — the failure to believe, to process, to respond — is the last expression of the grief that has dominated his narrative since 37:34. His revival (v.27: וַתְּחִי רוּחַ) through the wagons — material evidence that language alone could not carry — is one of the most humanly true moments in biblical narrative. The spirit that was dying for twenty-two years is rekindled not by a word but by wagons. And the chapter closes, as it began, with Joseph's name: "Joseph my son is still alive" — and with the word that was the chapter's theological pole star: חָי, "alive."
